text: Overtraining syndrome (OTS) is characterized by a long-term performance decrement resulting from a persisting imbalance between training-related and nontraining-related load and recovery.
OTS remains a diagnosis of exclusion due to the lack of a gold standard diagnostic test, requiring clinicians to rule out other medical conditions first.
OTS is multisystemic in nature, affecting hormonal, neurotransmitter, metabolic, psychological, cardiac, neurological, immunological, and musculoskeletal systems simultaneously.

Basal hormone levels, neurotransmitter levels, hormonal responses to stimuli, heart rate variability, psychological questionnaires, and exercise tests are all potentially diagnostic markers for overtraining syndrome.
Combinations of multiple biomarkers (as in the EROS diagnostic scores) may be required to diagnose OTS rather than any single marker alone.
Heart rate variability is among the potentially diagnostic tools for overtraining syndrome, making HRV monitoring a practical screening metric for coaches and athletes.

Current OTS diagnostic scores (EROS-CLINICAL, EROS-SIMPLIFIED, EROS-COMPLETE) have been developed primarily in male athletes and require validation in female athletes and larger samples before broad clinical application.
